column1: 4. Implications

14 of 17: The dual-phase ocular insert developed in this study meets its intended objectives
by providing an initial burst release of BS that mimics a loading dose, followed by sus-

column1: tained release for approximately 4 days, with negligible further release through Day 6
in one formulation (M1-7525). This design addresses the limitations of conventional eye
drops by maintaining therapeutic drug levels with a single application, thereby enhancing
patient adherence.

14 of 17: BS is a potent NSAID that typically requires once- or twice-daily administration in eye

column1: drop form. The developed insert, by contrast, could potentially replace multiple instillations
with a single post-surgical application. Although the current findings are based on in vitro
studies, the release profile supports further evaluation, including progression to ex vivo
testing to better simulate physiological conditions. Maintaining BS delivery for about

14 of 17: 4 days, with negligible further release to Day 6 in one formulation (M1-7525), may offer

column1: consistent anti-inflammatory action during the critical postoperative period. An additional
future step could involve evaluating a control insert containing only encapsulated drug
(without free BS) to further isolate the role of PLGA MPs in sustained release.

14 of 17: Zhang et al. [31] demonstrated that sustained BS delivery from a PLGA-based intraocu-

column1: lar implant effectively reduced inflammation and posterior capsule opacification in a rabbit

14 of 17: cataract model. While our insert differs by being topically applied and fully dissolvable,

column1: both systems rely on sustained NSAID release to support improved surgical outcomes.

14 of 17: This insert offers a minimally invasive alternative composed of soluble PVA and
biodegradable PLGA MPs that gradually erode, eliminating the need for surgical removal.
This feature is comparable to other biodegradable platforms such as the dexamethasone in-

column1: tracanalicular plug, with the added benefit of potential self-administration. The performance
of our insert compares favorably with established systems. For example, Ocusert achieved a
seven-day zero-order release of pilocarpine, and our formulation similarly provides extended
release of a modern NSAID using a simple matrix–particle system [32].

14 of 17: Although these results are based on in vitro conditions and physiological factors such
as tear turnover and blinking may alter drug release and retention, the observed mucoad-

column1: hesive properties suggest that the inserts could maintain sufficient residence time on the
ocular surface. This prolonged contact may help sustain drug levels and reduce the need

14 of 17: for frequent dosing. However, further studies using ex vivo ocular models or appropri-

column1: ate in vivo animal systems are necessary to evaluate pharmacokinetics and therapeutic
effectiveness under more biologically relevant conditions.
In summary,
14 of 17: the BS-loaded PLGA MP/PVA insert integrates both immediate and

column1: sustained drug release within a biodegradable platform. While the current findings are

14 of 17: encouraging, they remain limited to in vitro experiments. The demonstrated release charac-
teristics and insert performance support the need for continued investigation, particularly

column1: through ex vivo and subsequent in vivo studies, to assess the system’s clinical translation
potential and to investigate the long-term stability of

5. Conclusions

14 of 17: This study reports the development and in vitro evaluation of a dual-phase ocular

column1: insert designed to improve drug delivery in the postoperative setting. By incorporating
BS-loaded PLGA MPs into a dissolvable PVA matrix, the system enables an initial burst

14 of 17: release for an early therapeutic effect, followed by sustained drug delivery for up to 4 days,
